title: colophon
text:
colophon

\
Inspired by Jonathan W. Y. Gray this is a colophon page to detail some of what this website does and why.
\
This website is made using Quarto an ‘open-source scientific and technical publishing system’.
I like Quarto as it can be used to write slides, websites and books! If you are an academic once you get the hang of it it can fit easily into many of your work flows.
\
To make this website look how it does I have customized the base Quarto site layout quite significantly - and this remains a work in progress as I keep on tinkering.
I am using fonts I have chosen - FairfaxHD and Pilowlava - using the built in custom.scss. I also have a custom class to make highlights
\
As this is a static website it has no cookies and does not collect or store any data about who is visiting it.

title: About me
text:
About me

\





\
I have a PhD in Sociology from the LSE where my research looked at the information infrastructure of land registration in England and Wales and ‘offshore’ property ownership.
\
Before this I studied for a MPhil in Modern Soceity and Global Transformation at Cambridge University and BA in International Relations and Security Studies at the Univerity of Bradford.
\
After completing my PhD I moved onto Oxford University where I worked as a Research Associate in Finance and Geography as part of the CityNet Team.
\
Prior to starting at Warwick I was an Associate Lecturer in Geography and Data Visualisation at UCL where I taught Cartography and Data Visualisation to undergraduate and masters students. While teaching at UCL I also worked as CIVICA Open Social Science Researcher at the LSE.
\
I am currenlty working on the ESRC funded project ‘Can Digital Goods be Neutral? Evaluating OpenStreetMap’s equity through participatory data visualisation’ with Dr Carlos Cámara-Menoyo.
\
You can find out more about my publications here

title: Welcome
text:
Welcome

\
I am an Assistant Professor in the Centre for Interdisciplinary Methods at the University of Warwick. I teach on the Data Visulization MASc as well as several other course at CIM and Warwick.
\
I am a co-author of the Atlas of Finance and on the forthcoming book Doing Open Social Science: A Handbook for Researchers, along with other academic publications
\
I update this website sporadically with blog posts, tutorials and other tidbits. You can also follow me on Mastodon, bluesky or LinkedIn.
\
You can find more out about me here.
\

title: Highlights in Quarto
Changing fonts in Quarto
Line spacing in Emacs by making a new font
text:
Emacs does not have a native way in which to increase the line spacing across all documents. As someone that works a lot on large text documents in Emacs I find that text is sometimes just too close together, and having a bit of line spacing makes it much easier to work with long pages of text.
To get around the lack of line-spacing support I have created a hacky way to create a new font which includes extra white space above and below the text itself to create the impression of line spacing.
This code is taken/derived from: https://github.com/tam5/font-patcher
Fontforge needs to be installed from the website. via brew
then you can run this fontforge file.py

import os
import fontforge

input_file = 'directory/your_font.ttf'
output_dir = 'directory/your_new_font.ttf'

factor = 1.2 # line spacing to add

def adjust(font, attribute, factor):
    """Adjust an attribute of a font by a given factor."""
    original = getattr(font, attribute)
    new = int(getattr(font, attribute) * factor)

    setattr(font, attribute, new)


font = fontforge.open(input_file)

print('')
for prop in ['os2_winascent', 'os2_typoascent', 'hhea_ascent']:
    adjust(font, prop, factor)

for prop in ['os2_windescent', 'os2_typodescent', 'hhea_descent']:
    adjust(font, prop, factor * 2)

for attr in ['fontname', 'familyname', 'fullname']:
    value = "{} {}".format(getattr(font, attr), factor)
    setattr(font, attr, value)

font.copyright = "(c) {} Acme Corp. All Rights Reserved."

filename, extension = os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(input_file))
newFileName = "{}Patched {}{}".format(filename, factor, extension)

sfnt = {}
for el in font.sfnt_names:
    sfnt[el[1]] = el

sfnt["UniqueID"] = ('English (US)', 'UniqueID', font.fontname)
sfnt["Preferred Family"] = ('English (US)', 'Preferred Family', font.familyname)

font.sfnt_names = tuple(sfnt.values())

font.save(output_dir + "/" + newFileName)
font.generate(output_dir + "/" + newFileName)

And this is the result:

Before



After
